text: Convolutional neural networks (CNN) and Transformers have been widely applied to remote sensing change detection (CD) and achieved a high level of performance. However, there are still some limitations for these two types of models. Typically, the limited receptive field of models based on CNN restricts them from learning more global spatial contextual information of the image, and the costly computation of models based on Transformers restricts them from training and testing them on large amounts of data in an efficient way. Recently, Mamba architecture based on state space model, has been studied in various natural language processing tasks and can well compensate the above-mentioned two architectures. Inspired by these advantages of Mamba architecture, we introduce Mamba architecture into remote sensing change detection task and design a series of frameworks named MambaBCD, MambaSCD and MambaBDA for Binary Change Detection (BCD), Semantic Change Detection (SCD) and Building Damage Assessment (BDA) tasks, respectively. All the three frameworks utilize the Visual Mamba model as the encoder to make full use of the global spatial contextual information in the input images. In the change decoder available in all the frameworks, we propose three spatio-temporal relationship modeling mechanisms that could well integrate the advantages of Mamba architecture to realize the spatio-temporal interaction of multi-temporal feature and extract accurate change information from the input images. Experimental results on five public datasets show that the three frameworks are superior to the latest models based on CNN and Transformers without using any additional training techniques or implementations. Experiments about robustness of our proposed models to data degradation are also conducted and the source code of the models can be seen in https://github.com/ChenHongruixuan/MambaCD
explanation: The text contains concrete, domain-specific terminology and a real-sounding GitHub link, which is characteristic of human-written research prose; while some minor phrasing quirks exist, they don't strongly indicate AI generation.
